claim: Rectal temperature must exceed 43°C for at least 40 minutes to produce clinical heatstroke; acclimated working dogs routinely sustain >42°C post-exercise without illness.
stance_target: the 41°C diagnostic threshold is too conservative; 43°C is the operational danger threshold for field triage
scope_conditions: experimentally induced heatstroke in dogs (Shapiro et al. 1973) and naturally occurring cases in Israeli military working dogs
quoted_support: Shapiro et al. demonstrated in experimentally-induced heatstroke mongrel dogs that the rectal temperature should exceed 43°C for 40 minutes for development of clinical signs of heatstroke. The severity of the heatstroke was positively correlated to the maximal rectal temperature, as well as to its duration.

claim: High body weight (>15 kg), obesity, being a Labrador Retriever or brachycephalic breed, and lack of heat acclimation are independent risk factors for heatstroke death in dogs.
stance_target: breed and body composition are the primary biological predictors of fatal heat risk for lost dogs in the Algarve
scope_conditions: 54-dog retrospective study of naturally occurring heatstroke at Hebrew University, Israel
quoted_support: Several predisposing risk factors for heatstroke in dogs have been identified, including obesity, a high body weight (>15 kg), being of certain breed (e.g., Labrador retriever), lack of acclimation to heat stress, lack of fitness and exposure to a hot, often highly humid environment.

claim: Mortality rate of canine heatstroke is approximately 50% despite appropriate intensive treatment, making prevention superior to treatment.
stance_target: a lost dog entering heatstroke has only ~50% survival even with veterinary care — preventing sustained heat exposure is the only reliable intervention
scope_conditions: naturally occurring heatstroke, multiple referral hospital studies
quoted_support: despite early, appropriate body cooling, and intensive supportive treatment, with no available specific treatment to ameliorate the severe inflammatory and hemostatic derangements, the mortality rate is around 50%, similar to that of human heatstroke victims.

claim: Time from heat insult to veterinary care exceeding 90 minutes is an independent risk factor for death in canine heatstroke.
stance_target: rapid field response and cooling are critical within the first 90 minutes of observed heat collapse
scope_conditions: naturally occurring heatstroke dogs presented to referral hospital
quoted_support: Several significant risk factors for death were identified in dogs with heatstroke, including obesity, prolonged (>90 min) time-lag from the occurrence of the heat insult to presentation for veterinary care...

claim: Neurological signs in canine heatstroke include coma (40%), seizures (35%), and stupor (33%), appearing in the majority of severe cases.
stance_target: field identification of coma or seizures in a found dog in hot conditions constitutes a heat emergency requiring immediate veterinary transport
scope_conditions: 54 naturally occurring heatstroke dogs at Israeli referral hospital, 1999–2004
quoted_support: Neurological abnormalities are invariably present in dogs with clinical heatstroke, including coma (40%), seizures (35%) and stupor (33%).

claim: Dogs lack functional sweat glands except on paw pads, making the upper respiratory tract (nasal turbinates, oral mucosa, tongue) the primary evaporative cooling surface.
stance_target: any obstruction to panting (brachycephaly, exhaustion, collapse) directly disables thermal regulation
quoted_support: In dogs, the nasal turbinates provide a large surface area for water evaporation, from the moist mucous membranes, thereby playing an important role in heat dissipation through panting, while hypersalivation increases the evaporative efficiency through evaporation from the oral mucous membranes and the tongue.

claim: Hypoglycemia (blood glucose <47 mg/dL) at presentation is an independent death predictor in canine heatstroke, indicating systemic metabolic collapse.
stance_target: blood glucose is a useful prognostic field indicator if rapid testing equipment is available
scope_conditions: naturally occurring heatstroke dogs, Hebrew University
quoted_support: Several significant risk factors for death were identified in dogs with heatstroke, including obesity, prolonged (>90 min) time-lag from the occurrence of the heat insult to presentation for veterinary care, hypoglycemia (glucose <47 mg/dL) at presentation...
